text: This is a video presentation of laparoscopic supra-elevator posterior accentuation in a case of previous laparoscopic ovarian transposition. Our patient is a twenty eight year old lady with locally advanced rectal cancer with biopsy suggestive of moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with MRI suggestive of involvement of the anterior peritoneal reflection. The patient is planned for neoadjuvant chemoradiation prior to which she underwent laparoscopic ovarian transposition. The MRI done post neoadjuvant chemo radiation shows persistent involvement of the anterior peritoneal reflection and the patient is planned for laparoscopic supra levator posterior accentration.

The surgeon positions standard laparoscopic ports for rectal surgery and begins dissection below the inferior mesenteric vein, moving medially to laterally along the mesocolic fascia. The inferior mesenteric vein is clipped and divided near the DJ flexure. Adhesions of the sigmoid colon from previous ovarian transposition are lysed using a harmonic scalpel. The sigmoid colon is mobilized, retracted ventrally, and the medial peritoneal fold is incised. Dissection continues in the avascular plane below the inferior mesenteric artery, extending inferiorly into the pelvis posteriorly.

The surgeon bares the inferior mesenteric artery at its root, performing high ligation, clipping, and division. Dissection proceeds from medial to lateral along the mesocolic fascia until reaching the lateral peritoneal attachment. The lateral peritoneal cut is made, and the descending colon and splenic flexure are mobilized.

The surgeon scores the pelvic peritoneum along the iliac vessels, then dissects, clips, and divides the round ligament. The pelvic dissection continues to create the para-rectal and para-bicycle spaces, followed by the dissection of the left ureter extending to the UV junction.

The surgeon continues the ureteric dissection towards the uretero-vesical junction and encounters the uterine artery. The uterine artery, which separates the para-rectal and para-vesical spaces, is clipped and divided. The ureteric dissection then proceeds until reaching the uretero-vesical junction.
The surgeon incises the uterovesical fold of peritoneum, separating the bladder from the lower uterine segment and cervix. The same procedure is performed on the opposite side.

The surgeon begins dissecting the ureter near the pelvic inlet, followed by dissection, clipping, and division of the round ligament. The ureteric dissection continues further into the pelvis until the uterine artery is identified.

The surgeon clips and divides the opposite uterine artery, then proceeds with the posterior dissection until reaching the pelvic floor. Using a monopolar cautery, the antevaginal wall is incised to open the vaginal vault. The lateral dissection of the rectum begins, with care taken to preserve the hypogastric nerve, as indicated by an arrow.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection reaching the pelvic floor, then incises the posterior vaginal wall to finalize the vaginal cut. Following this, dissection proceeds in the rectovaginal septum to fully mobilize the rectum.

The surgeon completes the total mesorectal excision by fully mobilizing the rectum down to the pelvic floor. An endostepler is used to divide the distal end of the rectal specimen. The vaginal vault is then sutured with a V-lock suture.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a circular stapler, confirming the alignment of the colon before firing the stapler. JP drains are inserted into the pelvis, and a covering ileostomy is performed.

The final list of pathology revealed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma with negative proximal and distal resection margins with negative circumferential resection margin and three out of fifteen lymph nodes positive
